name: Hydrogen
name_origin: From Greek 'hydro' (water) and 'genes' (forming), meaning 'water-former', coined by Antoine Lavoisier in 1783.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Hydrogen is the most abundant chemical substance in the universe, comprising about 75% of all baryonic mass. On Earth it is found primarily bound in water and hydrocarbons; free H2 gas is rare in the atmosphere (~0.5 ppm).

compounds.name: Water
compounds.summary: The universal solvent, essential for all known life and ubiquitous on Earth's surface.

compounds.name: Ammonia
compounds.summary: A pungent gas synthesized industrially via the Haber-Bosch process and used as a fertilizer feedstock.

compounds.name: Methane
compounds.summary: The simplest hydrocarbon and the principal component of natural gas.

compounds.name: Hydrogen chloride
compounds.summary: A strong mineral acid (when dissolved in water) used widely in industrial chemistry and metal processing.

compounds.name: Hydrogen peroxide
compounds.summary: A powerful oxidizer used as a disinfectant, bleaching agent, and rocket propellant.

uses: Feedstock for ammonia production via the Haber-Bosch process
Hydrogenation of vegetable oils to produce margarine and shortenings
Rocket fuel (liquid hydrogen) for cryogenic upper stages
Fuel cells for electric vehicles and stationary power
Coolant in large electrical generators due to its high thermal conductivity
reactivity_summary: Hydrogen is highly flammable in air, burning with a nearly invisible flame to produce water. It reacts vigorously with halogens and oxygen, and at elevated temperatures with many metals to form hydrides. Pure H2 gas is otherwise relatively unreactive at room temperature due to its strong H-H bond.
isotopes_summary: Hydrogen has three naturally occurring isotopes: protium (1H, ~99.98%), deuterium (2H or D, ~0.02%), and trace radioactive tritium (3H, half-life 12.32 years). Deuterium is used in heavy water moderators and NMR spectroscopy; tritium is used in self-luminous markers and fusion research.

citations.source: Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Hydrogen
CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
